ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried rice class (FOODON_00002694); no specific Nasi Goreng Seafood entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers fried rice as a category; no seafood-specific nasi goreng sub-code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Nasi Goreng Seafood or fried rice dish at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Nasi Goreng Seafood as typically served at a Malaysian or Singaporean hawker stall or casual restaurant, weighing approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Goreng (Fried Rice with Egg) and analogous seafood fried rice entries — IMR Malaysia MyFCD and HPB Singapore Foodo composite
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD); Health Promotion Board Singapore — Foodo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single exact entry for Nasi Goreng Seafood was found. Values are adapted from IMR MyFCD 'Nasi Goreng' (plain fried rice with egg) and HPB Foodo 'Fried Rice with Seafood' entries, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Southeast Asian fried rice dishes. Protein and fat values adjusted upward to reflect seafood component (prawns, squid). Sodium reflects typical soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning levels documented in MyFCD composite dishes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — white rice and fried rice analogues; University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Nasi Goreng Seafood was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 64 is estimated from the closest analogous food: white jasmine rice fried with egg and soy sauce (GI ~64, medium category), consistent with published values for Southeast Asian fried rice in Atkinson et al. (2021) International Tables of GI and GL. The addition of seafood protein and fat is expected to modestly lower the effective GI; the value of 64 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 78.75) / 100 = 50.4, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 22.5 × 3.5 = 78.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: A single 350 g serving of Nasi Goreng Seafood contributes approximately 1680 mg sodium, representing 73–84% of the WHO-recommended daily limit of 2000 mg, and should be consumed infrequently by individuals managing hypertension; requesting reduced-sauce preparation can meaningfully lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index and high carbohydrate load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 64 (medium) and a glycemic load of ~50 per standard serving, Nasi Goreng Seafood can cause a significant postprandial glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, pair with non-starchy vegetables, and monitor blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from cooking oil and egg yolk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content and moderate saturated fat (approximately 3.85 g per serving) are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption frequency, choose preparations with less oil, and balance this dish within a diet low in total saturated fat and sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Goreng Seafood is a popular Southeast Asian fried rice dish widely served in Malaysia and Singapore. It is prepared by stir-frying pre-cooked white jasmine rice in a hot wok with cooking oil, garlic, shallots, egg, and a mixed seafood combination typically including prawns, squid, and fish cake. The dish is seasoned with soy sauce, oyster sauce, and chilli paste or fresh chilli, giving it a savoury, slightly smoky, and mildly spicy flavour profile with a tender yet slightly chewy texture. The wok hei (breath of the wok) imparted by high-heat cooking is a defining characteristic. Nasi Goreng Seafood is a complete one-dish meal providing carbohydrates from rice, protein from seafood and egg, and moderate fat from cooking oil. It is a staple at Malaysian and Singaporean hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and casual restaurants. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g serving provides approximately 567 kcal, 25 g protein, 16.8 g fat, and 78.75 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is high due to soy sauce and oyster sauce, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension. The medium glycemic index (~64) and high glycemic load per serving (~50) are relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish contains common allergens including crustaceans, molluscs, fish, egg, soy, and wheat. It is generally considered halal when prepared without pork-derived ingredients.

confidence_reason: No single exact entry for Nasi Goreng Seafood was found in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR MyFCD and HPB Foodo fried rice entries with seafood adjustment, and GI is estimated from the closest analogous fried rice entry in the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence reflects the use of adapted rather than exact source data.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD or HPB Foodo source entries for fried rice dishes. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source entries used. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD or HPB Foodo source entries. All other -1 values reflect nutrients not reported in the adapted source data. No core macro is zero; all four core macros are positive and nutritionally consistent with a fried rice dish containing seafood, egg, and cooking oil. GI source is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fried rice data; direct GI measurement for Nasi Goreng Seafood is not available in the approved source set. Halal status is conditional on preparation without pork-derived ingredients, which is typical but not guaranteed at all establishments. The two field survey source restaurants (Singapore Banana Leaf Restaurant and Baba Eating House) are noted as context for the Singapore market presence of this dish but are not used as primary nutrient evidence.
